agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The courier assignment system has an inherent tension between strong consistency (no double-assignment) and high availability (survive node failures) — a classic CAP theorem conflict. For a 2-engineer team, the architecture must ruthlessly prioritize simplicity. The recommended approach uses a single PostgreSQL instance with synchronous replication to a hot standby, an in-memory assignment lock via SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, and a thin application layer. The 200ms latency target is achievable with this design since PostgreSQL row-level locking is sub-millisecond, leaving ample budget for application logic and network hops. We explicitly choose CP over AP: during a failover window (seconds, not minutes, with automated failover), new assignments will fail rather than risk double-assignment.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Assignment Service","responsibility":"Receives order-placed events, selects the best available courier, and atomically assigns them. Stateless horizontally-scaled instances behind a load balancer.","boundaries":"Owns the assignment decision logic and courier availability state. Does NOT own order lifecycle or courier profile management."},{"name":"PostgreSQL Primary + Hot Standby","responsibility":"Source of truth for courier availability and active assignments. Provides transactional consistency via row-level locking. Hot standby with synchronous replication for durability; async promotion via Patroni or pg_auto_failover for HA.","boundaries":"Owns courier_assignments and courier_availability tables. Does not store order details beyond order_id foreign key."},{"name":"Event Ingress (Message Queue)","responsibility":"Receives order-placed events. Provides at-least-once delivery semantics. Can be a simple Redis Stream or SQS queue — nothing exotic.","boundaries":"Decouples order service from assignment service. Does not participate in the consistency boundary."},{"name":"Health Monitor / Failover Manager","responsibility":"Monitors PostgreSQL primary health. Promotes hot standby on failure. Patroni or pg_auto_failover — battle-tested, minimal ops burden.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern only. No business logic."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Event Ingress","contract":"OrderPlaced event: {order_id, pickup_location, delivery_location, time_window}","coupling_type":"async, event-driven"},{"from":"Event Ingress","to":"Assignment Service","contract":"Pulls/receives OrderPlaced events, at-least-once delivery","coupling_type":"async, consumer-pull"},{"from":"Assignment Service","to":"PostgreSQL","contract":"BEGIN → SELECT courier FROM available_couriers WHERE status='available' ORDER BY distance LIMIT 1 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED → INSERT assignment → UPDATE courier status → COMMIT","coupling_type":"synchronous, transactional"},{"from":"Assignment Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"AssignmentCompleted event or callback: {order_id, courier_id, estimated_pickup}","coupling_type":"async, event-driven"}],"data_flow":"OrderPlaced event → Event Ingress → Assignment Service consumes event → Single PostgreSQL transaction: lock best available courier row (SKIP LOCKED prevents contention), insert assignment, mark courier unavailable → Emit AssignmentCompleted event back to Order Service. Total hot path: 1 DB round-trip (~5-15ms) + application logic (~5ms) + network (~10ms) = well under 200ms.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED","rationale":"PostgreSQL's SKIP LOCKED is purpose-built for work-queue patterns. Concurrent assignment requests automatically skip already-locked courier rows, eliminating double-assignment without application-level distributed locking. This is the key insight — the database IS the lock manager."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox (optional, phase 2)","rationale":"If event delivery guarantees matter, the assignment and outbound event can be written in the same transaction, then a poller publishes events. For a 2-person team, start without this — emit events after commit and accept rare missed events."},{"pattern":"Hot Standby with Synchronous Replication","rationale":"Synchronous replication to one standby ensures no committed assignment is lost on failover. Adds ~1-3ms latency per write — acceptable within the 200ms budget."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Assignment","rationale":"At-least-once event delivery means the assignment operation must be idempotent. Use order_id as a natural idempotency key with a UNIQUE constraint on active assignments per order."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAvailability (aggregate root: courier_id, status, current_location, last_updated)","Assignment (aggregate root: assignment_id, order_id, courier_id, assigned_at, status)"]},{"name":"Order Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Order (externally owned — we only consume OrderPlaced events)"]},{"name":"Courier Profile","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierProfile (externally owned — name, vehicle type, ratings — not our concern)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes OrderPlaced events. Courier Assignment consumes them. Order Management does not depend on assignment internals."},{"upstream":"Courier Profile","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Conformist. Courier Assignment conforms to courier IDs and basic profile data from the Courier Profile context. An ACL is overkill for a 2-person team — a shared courier_id is sufficient."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single PostgreSQL with hot standby instead of distributed database","gains":"Simplicity, strong consistency via row-level locking, well-understood operational model, 2 engineers can manage it, sub-millisecond lock acquisition","sacrifices":"Single-region write path, failover window of 5-30 seconds where assignments fail, write throughput ceiling (~10K TPS which is far beyond logistics scale)","alternatives_considered":["CockroachDB / Spanner — distributed consistency but massive operational complexity for 2 engineers","Redis RedLock — fast but not truly consistent (Kleppmann's analysis), poor durability story","Application-level optimistic locking — more code, retry storms under contention"]},{"decision":"CP over AP — fail assignments during failover rather than risk double-assignment","gains":"Never assign two orders to the same courier. Business correctness preserved. Couriers don't receive conflicting instructions.","sacrifices":"During a failover window (5-30s), new orders queue up and experience delayed assignment. With automated failover (Patroni), this window is minimized.","alternatives_considered":["AP with conflict resolution — allow double-assignment then resolve. More complex, confusing for couriers, requires compensation logic. Wrong trade-off for this domain."]},{"decision":"SKIP LOCKED instead of application-level distributed lock","gains":"Zero additional infrastructure. Lock contention handled by PostgreSQL's battle-tested lock manager. No Redis/Zookeeper dependency. Concurrent requests naturally fan out to different couriers.","sacrifices":"Tied to PostgreSQL. If you ever need to move to a different database, this pattern doesn't port directly.","alternatives_considered":["Advisory locks — more flexible but harder to reason about","Distributed lock (Redis/etcd) — additional infrastructure, additional failure mode, 2 engineers shouldn't manage this"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Courier selection algorithm","evolution_path":"Currently ORDER BY distance LIMIT 1. Can evolve to scoring function (distance, rating, vehicle type, workload balance) without architectural change — it's just a different ORDER BY or scoring query."},{"area":"Event ingress technology","evolution_path":"Start with Redis Streams or SQS. Can swap to Kafka if volume demands it. Assignment Service is decoupled from the queue technology via a thin consumer interface."},{"area":"Multi-region expansion","evolution_path":"Partition by city/region. Each region gets its own PostgreSQL primary. Courier assignment is inherently local (you don't assign a courier in NYC to a Paris order), so geographic sharding is natural and doesn't break the consistency model."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"PostgreSQL as consistency backbone","reason":"The entire double-assignment prevention relies on PostgreSQL row-level locking. This is intentionally rigid — it's the core invariant. Changing the database requires re-solving the consistency problem."},{"area":"Synchronous write path for assignment","reason":"Assignment must be a synchronous transaction. Cannot be made eventually consistent without violating the no-double-assignment requirement. This is a correct rigidity."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Failover window causes order assignment delays during PostgreSQL primary failure","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use Patroni or pg_auto_failover for automated failover (5-15s window). Queue absorbs orders during the window. Alert on failover events. Conduct quarterly failover drills."},{"risk":"Hot courier contention — all requests try to lock the same 'best' courier","severity":"low","mitigation":"SKIP LOCKED inherently handles this: if courier A is locked, the next request gets courier B. No retry needed. Under extreme contention, the Nth request gets the Nth-best courier — graceful degradation."},{"risk":"Courier location staleness leads to suboptimal assignments","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Couriers push location updates every 10-30 seconds. Accept that assignment is 'good enough' not 'optimal'. Stale location doesn't violate correctness, only optimality."},{"risk":"Synchronous replication latency spike under load","severity":"low","mitigation":"Monitor replication lag. If standby becomes slow, the write latency increases but 200ms budget has ~180ms of headroom. Can temporarily switch to async replication if durability trade-off is acceptable during an incident."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Week 1-2): PostgreSQL schema with courier_availability and assignments tables. Assignment Service with a single endpoint that runs the SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED transaction. Simple distance-based courier selection. Integration test that proves two concurrent requests never get the same courier. Phase 2 (Week 3-4): Hot standby setup with Patroni. Event-driven ingress from order service. Monitoring and alerting. Phase 3 (future): Scoring algorithm improvements, transactional outbox, multi-region.","files_to_create_or_modify":["migrations/001_create_courier_availability.sql","migrations/002_create_assignments.sql","src/assignment-service/handler.ts (or .py — single file, <200 lines)","src/assignment-service/repository.ts (PostgreSQL queries, including the SKIP LOCKED query)","src/assignment-service/consumer.ts (event ingress consumer)","tests/concurrent-assignment.test.ts (THE critical test — spawn 10 concurrent assignment requests, assert no courier is double-assigned)","infra/patroni.yml (failover configuration)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Keep it boring. One service, one database, one transaction. The entire assignment hot path should be understandable by reading a single function. No microservices, no event sourcing, no CQRS — those are for problems you don't have. The concurrent assignment test is the most important artifact — it proves your core invariant.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use a distributed lock (Redis RedLock, etcd) — it adds a failure mode and is not as consistent as you think","Do NOT split this into microservices — 2 engineers cannot operate multiple services plus databases plus message queues","Do NOT use eventual consistency for the assignment decision — 'resolve conflicts later' means confused couriers and broken trust","Do NOT optimize courier selection before proving the consistency invariant — get locking right first, then improve the scoring","Do NOT use async/background assignment — the 200ms SLA means synchronous is fine and simpler"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["PostgreSQL SKIP LOCKED is the right primitive for this problem — battle-tested, minimal complexity, perfect fit for work-queue assignment patterns","CP over AP is the correct trade-off — double-assignment is a business-breaking bug, delayed assignment during failover is an acceptable inconvenience","A 2-engineer team must optimize for simplicity above all else — one database, one service, boring technology"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact failover window duration depends on infrastructure setup (cloud provider managed PostgreSQL vs self-managed Patroni) — needs validation during implementation","Whether Redis Streams or SQS is better for event ingress depends on existing infrastructure — either works"],"assumptions":["Order volume is in the hundreds-to-low-thousands per minute range (typical logistics), not millions — if this assumption is wrong, the single-PostgreSQL design needs revisiting","Courier availability updates (location, status) are coming from a separate system and written to the same PostgreSQL instance","The 200ms latency target is measured from event receipt to assignment commit, not end-to-end from customer button click"]}}
